perturbation.original_text: EnCana hereby acknowledges that it is aware, and that it has advised or will advise its Representatives who are informed as to the matters that arc the subject of this agreement, that the United States securities laws may prohibit any person who has material, nonpublic information concerning the matters that are the subject of this agreement from purchasing or selling securities of a company that may be a party to a transaction of the type contemplated by this agreement or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.changed_text: EnCana hereby acknowledges that it is aware, and that it has advised or will advise its Representatives who are informed as to the matters that arc the subject of this agreement. Each party acknowledges and agrees that each reserves the right, in its sole and absolute discretion, to reject any and all proposals and to terminate discussions and negotiations with the other at any time, that the United States securities laws may prohibit any person who has material, nonpublic information concerning the matters that are the subject of this agreement from purchasing or selling securities of a company that may be a party to a transaction of the type contemplated by this agreement or from communicating such information to any other person under circumstances in which it is reasonably foreseeable that such person is likely to purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the Securities Law acknowledgement into the middle of a clause regarding the rejection of proposals and termination of discussions obscures its importance and makes it less likely to be noticed and followed, increasing the risk of insider trading violations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device or contrivance in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By burying the acknowledgement of securities laws within a clause about terminating discussions, the contract undermines the prominence of this warning, potentially leading to violations of Rule 10b-5.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The modified text buries the acknowledgment of securities laws within a clause about terminating discussions, potentially leading to violations of Rule 10b-5. This is a structural flaw that affects the clarity and enforceability of the securities law acknowledgement. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the new position of the acknowledgement makes it less likely to be noticed and followed. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By obscuring the securities law acknowledgment, the revised contract arguably undermines its prominence, potentially facilitating a violation of Rule 10b-5, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. The modification results in a clear non-compliance with the intention behind securities regulations.

perturbation.original_text: The validity and interpretation of this letter agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of Colorado without regard to conflicts-of-law rules or principles.
perturbation.changed_text: The validity and interpretation of this letter agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the procedure for communications outlined in section 6 without regard to conflicts-of-law rules or principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law clause to point to the communication procedures introduces ambiguity and contradicts fundamental contract law principles, as communication procedures do not govern the interpretation or enforcement of a contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: C.R.S. § 13-1-120 states that contract interpretation and validity are subject to applicable substantive law, not procedural rules. Substituting communication procedures for governing law directly contradicts the statute and invalidates the clause.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the laws of the State of Colorado govern the contract's validity, interpretation, and enforcement. The changed text replaces this with 'the procedure for communications outlined in section 6.' The Law Explanation states that C.R.S. § 13-1-120 requires contract interpretation and validity to be subject to applicable substantive law, not procedural rules. Because the changed text substitutes a procedural element (communication procedures) for substantive law, it contradicts the statute, invalidating the clause as explained in the Law Explanation and as a Structural Flaw, according to the definitions.

perturbation.original_text: EnCana agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this letter agreement by EnCana or any of its Representatives and that TBI shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as well as reimbursement for legal and other expenses as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this letter agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity. In the event of litigation concerning this letter agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines in a final nonappealable order that a party has breached this letter agreement, then such party shall be liable for and pay the other party's reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom or review thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: EnCana agrees that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this letter agreement by EnCana or any of its Representatives. In the event of litigation concerning this letter agreement, if a court of competent jurisdiction determines in a final nonappealable order that a party has breached this letter agreement, then such party shall not be liable for and pay the other party's reasonable legal fees and expenses incurred in connection with such litigation, including any appeal therefrom or review thereof and that TBI shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as well as reimbursement for legal and other expenses as a remedy for any such breach. Such remedies shall not be deemed to be the exclusive remedies for a breach of this letter agreement, but shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or equity.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing a negative constraint in the fee shifting agreement while maintaining the right to seek equitable relief introduces a contradiction. While equitable relief is still possible, the party may not be able to recover legal fees, contrary to standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly a statute, RPC 1.5(a) deals with reasonableness of fees. Altering the fee provision as described means that the party can seek equitable relief, but not recover any fees for it. If the conduct being litigated is extremely harmful, and the company has to pay $500k in fees to get an injunction to stop that harm, the contract states the company can't recoup those fees. This can be seen as highly unreasonable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stating that in the event of litigation, a party that breached the agreement "shall not be liable for and pay the other party's reasonable legal fees and expenses," while simultaneously allowing the other party to seek equitable relief, including legal fees. The Law Explanation argues this could be viewed as unreasonable. Although the snippets provided do not give specific statutory information, the Law Explanation points to RPC 1.5(a) dealing with reasonableness of fees, and argues that being unable to recoup fees can be highly unreasonable, creating a legal contradiction as defined.

perturbation.original_text: Until the first anniversary of the date hereof, EnCana will not, nor will it permit any of its subsidiaries, directors, officers or employees to, directly or indirectly, solicit or hire the services, as employee, consultant or otherwise, of any employee of TBI or its subsidiaries with whom EnCana had contact in connection with its consideration of the Transaction or who became known to EnCana specifically in connection with its consideration of the Transaction, except that this provision shall not prohibit the hiring of any such employee (i) who responds to any public advertising for employment without any other direct or indirect solicitation or (ii) whose employment by TBI or its subsidiary has been terminated prior to the commencement of discussions with such employee.
perturbation.changed_text: Until the first anniversary of the date hereof, EnCana will not, nor will it permit any of its subsidiaries, directors, officers or employees to, directly or indirectly, solicit or hire the services, as employee, consultant or otherwise, of any employee of TBI or its subsidiaries with whom EnCana had contact in connection with its consideration of the Transaction or who became known to EnCana specifically in connection with its consideration of the Transaction. (a) Unless and until a definitive agreement between the parties with respect to any Transaction has been executed and delivered, neither TBI nor EnCana will be under any legal obligation of any kind whatsoever with respect to a Transaction by virtue of this letter or any written or oral expression with respect to such a Transaction by any of its Representatives except for the matters specifically agreed to in this letter agreement. Each party further agrees that neither party shall have any obligation to authorize or pursue with the other party any Transaction. Each party acknowledges and agrees that each reserves the right, in its sole and absolute discretion, to reject any and all proposals and to terminate discussions and negotiations with the other at any time. , except that this provision shall not prohibit the hiring of any such employee (i) who responds to any public advertising for employment without any other direct or indirect solicitation or (ii) whose employment by TBI or its subsidiary has been terminated prior to the commencement of discussions with such employee.
perturbation.explanation: By inserting the 'no obligation' clause within the non-solicitation clause and splitting the exception, the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement is weakened. It creates ambiguity about whether the 'no obligation' clause applies to the non-solicitation clause. It also makes the exceptions harder to understand.
perturbation.law_explanation: C.R.S. § 8-2-113 permits non-solicitation agreements, but requires them to be reasonable. Obscuring the non-solicitation clause with unrelated information and splitting it makes its terms less clear and potentially less reasonable, inviting legal challenges to its enforceability.
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw creates a legal contradiction. The insertion of the 'no obligation' clause within the non-solicitation clause weakens the enforceability of the non-solicitation agreement by creating ambiguity. This ambiguity about whether the 'no obligation' clause applies to the non-solicitation clause can lead to legal challenges to its enforceability. The LLM law explanation notes that C.R.S. § 8-2-113 permits non-solicitation agreements, but requires them to be reasonable, and that obscuring the clause makes it less clear and potentially less reasonable.
